questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of progressive dyspnoea on exertion, orthopnoea, and ankle swelling. He has no chest pain or cough. On examination, his jugular venous pressure is elevated at 8 cm, he has bibasal fine crackles, and pitting oedema to the knees. Heart sounds are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF)
questions.explanation: The combination of orthopnoea, raised JVP, bibasal crackles, and peripheral oedema is classic for biventricular failure. Crackles indicate pulmonary congestion, raised JVP and oedema indicate right heart failure. While HFpEF can present similarly, HFrEF is more common in this age group and sex, and the lack of risk factors (e.g., hypertension) makes HFrEF the most likely diagnosis initially.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known ischaemic cardiomyopathy and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% presents for routine follow-up. She is on ramipril 5 mg daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, and furosemide 40 mg daily. She has no dyspnoea at rest, can climb one flight of stairs without stopping, and has no peripheral oedema. Her heart rate is 58 bpm, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Well-compensated chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction
questions.explanation: The patient has known HFrEF on optimal medical therapy, is asymptomatic at rest (NYHA II), and has normal vital signs without signs of congestion. This is consistent with stable, well-compensated HFrEF. Decompensation would show worse symptoms or signs of fluid overload. The absence of a murmur and stable blood pressure makes severe aortic stenosis unlikely.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%) presents with acute dyspnoea, confusion, and cold, clammy extremities. His heart rate is 110 bpm, blood pressure 80/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 30/min, oxygen saturation 88% on room air. Jugular venous pressure is elevated at 10 cm, and there are bibasal crackles. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Cardiogenic shock due to acute decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
questions.explanation: The combination of hypotension, cold peripheries, confusion, elevated JVP, and pulmonary oedema in a patient with known HFrEF is diagnostic of cardiogenic shock. Massive PE would typically have a clear chest (no crackles) with signs of right heart strain. Tamponade has muffled heart sounds and pulsus paradoxus. Tension pneumothorax has absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation. Septic shock usually has warm peripheries initially.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%) on ramipril, bisoprolol, and furosemide presents with a 2-day history of palpitations and worsening breathlessness. On examination, her heart rate is 140 bpm and irregularly irregular, blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, and there are bibasal crackles. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Sinus tachycardia due to decompensated heart failure
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response complicating heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
questions.explanation: The irregularly irregular pulse and ECG findings confirm atrial fibrillation. In a patient with HFrEF, new-onset AF with rapid ventricular response is a common precipitant of decompensation. VT would be regular and broad-complex. SVT is regular and narrow-complex. Atrial flutter often has a regular rate. Sinus tachycardia would be regular and the ECG would show sinus rhythm.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35%) presents with a 1-month history of early satiety, nausea, and abdominal distension. He has mildly swollen ankles. He denies dyspnoea or chest pain. On examination, his jugular venous pressure is elevated at 6 cm, the liver is enlarged 4 cm below the costal margin, and there is mild ascites. His lungs are clear. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Congestive hepatopathy due to right heart failure from heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
questions.explanation: This patient has signs of right heart failure (elevated JVP, hepatomegaly, ascites, pedal oedema) without pulmonary congestion. In chronic HFrEF, biventricular failure can lead to hepatic congestion, presenting with gastrointestinal symptoms. Cirrhosis would have signs of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema) and usually no JVP elevation. Malignancy would have weight loss and possible mass on exam.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea and bilateral ankle swelling. He has no known cardiac history. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of heart failure?
questions.explanation: NICE CG187 recommends NT-proBNP as the first-line test in suspected heart failure. If elevated, a referral for echocardiography is indicated. Echocardiogram is diagnostic but not the initial test. ECG and CXR may show abnormalities but are not sufficient for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known HFrEF (LVEF 40%) on an ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker reports mild fatigue but no worsening breathlessness. Which investigation is most appropriate to reassess left ventricular systolic function?
questions.explanation: Echocardiography is the gold standard for assessing LVEF. NICE recommends echocardiogram for diagnosis and reassessment if symptoms change. NT-proBNP can be used for monitoring but echo is more definitive for systolic function. Cardiac MRI is more sensitive but not routinely indicated.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known HFrEF presents with sudden severe dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and pink frothy sputum. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of acute pulmonary oedema?
questions.explanation: In acute pulmonary oedema, chest X-ray shows bilateral interstitial and alveolar opacities, confirming the diagnosis. NT-proBNP is elevated but not specific. ECG and ABG may show abnormalities but are not diagnostic. Echocardiogram can assess LV function but is not urgent for confirmation of pulmonary oedema.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with severe HFrEF (LVEF 25%) on optimal medical therapy develops palpitations and pre-syncope. Which investigation is most appropriate to diagnose the likely complication?
questions.explanation: Patients with HFrEF are at risk of ventricular arrhythmias. Palpitations and pre-syncope suggest arrhythmia; 24-hour Holter monitoring is the first-line investigation to capture rhythm disturbances. Echocardiogram may show thrombus but not rhythm. Electrophysiology study is reserved for persistent arrhythmias.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a history of Hodgkin lymphoma treated with doxorubicin 10 years ago presents with dyspnoea on exertion and fatigue. She has no peripheral oedema. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy?
questions.explanation: Echocardiography is the first-line test to assess left ventricular systolic function in suspected anthracycline cardiomyopathy. NT-proBNP may be elevated but is not diagnostic. Cardiac MRI is more sensitive for detecting early changes but is not routinely first-line due to availability and cost.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is newly diagnosed with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35%). He is euvolemic with no prior treatment. Which is the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying therapy?
questions.explanation: Ramipril (ACE inhibitor) is first-line for mortality and morbidity in HFrEF. Diuretics only relieve symptoms; beta-blockers are added after ACE; MRAs are third-line; digoxin is not first-line for sinus rhythm.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with HFrEF (LVEF 30%) remains breathless on minimal exertion (NYHA III) despite ramipril 10 mg daily and bisoprolol 5 mg daily. She is in sinus rhythm. Which add-on therapy is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Spironolactone (MRA) is recommended as third-line after ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker in symptomatic HFrEF. ARNI is considered if still symptomatic after MRA; ivabradine requires sinus rate >70; digoxin only for AF; CRT is for wide QRS.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known HFrEF presents with acute pulmonary oedema, BP 90/60 mmHg, and rapid atrial fibrillation (ventricular rate 140 bpm). Which drug is contraindicated in this setting?
questions.explanation: Verapamil (non-dihydropyridine CCB) is contraindicated in HFrEF due to its negative inotropic effects. Digoxin and amiodarone are safe for rate control; bisoprolol may be used cautiously but is not first-line in acute decompensation.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 30%) on optimal medical therapy (ramipril, bisoprolol, spironolactone) remains NYHA III. ECG shows left bundle branch block with QRS duration 160 ms. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: CRT is indicated for symptomatic HFrEF with LBBB and QRS ≥150 ms despite optimal medical therapy. ICD alone does not improve symptoms; ivabradine requires sinus rhythm; digoxin and diuretic changes are not definitive.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with HFrEF (LVEF 40%) and severe aortic stenosis (valve area 0.7 cm²) is being considered for pharmacological therapy. Which drug is relatively contraindicated?
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril) can cause hypotension in severe aortic stenosis due to fixed outflow obstruction. Beta-blockers, MRAs, furosemide, and digoxin are safer options, though all require monitoring.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive breathlessness and peripheral oedema. Examination shows raised JVP, basal crackles, and a displaced apex beat. ECG reveals left bundle branch block. He is not in acute distress. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: According to NICE guidelines, in new suspected heart failure, echocardiography is the key first diagnostic test to confirm left ventricular systolic dysfunction. Empirical treatment should not be started before a definitive diagnosis is made unless there is acute decompensation.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known HFrEF (LVEF 35%) is on ramipril 5 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 5 mg once daily, and furosemide 40 mg once daily. She is breathless on moderate exertion but stable. BP 105/70 mmHg, eGFR 50 mL/min/1.73m². What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Up-titrate bisoprolol to 7.5 mg once daily
Increase furosemide to 80 mg once daily
questions.explanation: NICE recommends adding a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (e.g., spironolactone) in patients with LVEF ≤40% who remain symptomatic despite ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker therapy. Up-titrating bisoprolol is reasonable but the low blood pressure makes further beta-blockade risky. Dapagliflozin is an option but MRA is first-line in this setting.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known HFrEF (LVEF 30%) on ramipril, bisoprolol, and spironolactone presents with severe dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and SaO₂ 85% on room air. BP 90/60 mmHg, HR 110 bpm. Crepitations to apices. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: This patient has acute pulmonary oedema with severe hypoxia. CPAP (non-invasive ventilation) is the most urgent intervention to improve oxygenation and reduce work of breathing. IV furosemide is also important but comes after immediate respiratory support. IV dobutamine may be considered if there is cardiogenic shock, but hypotension is not severe.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with HFrEF (LVEF 25%) on optimal medical therapy develops worsening oedema and abdominal distension. She has atrial fibrillation (rate 80–100 bpm) and is anticoagulated with warfarin. She complains of nausea and mild abdominal pain. BP 100/65 mmHg. JVP elevated, hepatomegaly. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In a patient on warfarin with signs of right heart failure and nausea, hepatic congestion may impair warfarin metabolism, leading to a supratherapeutic INR and increased bleeding risk. Checking INR is the quickest and most immediate action to guide anticoagulation management. Abdominal ultrasound and LFTs are useful but not the first step.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 40%) on ramipril, bisoprolol, and spironolactone is well controlled. He has type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3 (eGFR 45 mL/min/1.73m²). What is the most appropriate next step to further reduce mortality and hospitalisation?
options: Increase spironolactone to 50 mg once daily
questions.explanation: The DAPA-HF trial showed that dapagliflozin reduces mortality and heart failure hospitalisations in patients with HFrEF, regardless of diabetes. NICE recommends it as an option for people with LVEF ≤40%. Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with heart rate >70 bpm on beta-blocker. ICD is for LVEF ≤35%. Increasing spironolactone is not supported by evidence at this dose.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and 3 kg weight gain. He has no significant past medical history. On examination, his pulse is 110 bpm, blood pressure 140/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, and oxygen saturations 92% on room air. He has bibasal crackles up to the mid-zones, raised jugular venous pressure, and pitting oedema to the knees. Which single finding would most strongly indicate an immediate life-threatening complication?
options: Bibasal crackles up to the mid-zones
questions.explanation: Bibasal crackles up to the mid-zones indicate acute pulmonary oedema, which is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate intervention. The other options suggest fluid overload but are not as acutely dangerous.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with chronic HFrEF (LVEF 30%) has been stable on bisoprolol 5 mg daily, ramipril 5 mg daily, and furosemide 40 mg daily. She now presents with a 4-day history of increasing breathlessness and orthopnoea. She has gained 2.5 kg since her last review 2 weeks ago. On examination, JVP is elevated 6 cm, her blood pressure is 100/65 mmHg, and she has bibasal fine crackles. Which single finding should prompt urgent escalation (e.g., acute heart failure assessment)?
questions.explanation: A weight gain of >2 kg in 3 days is a red flag for fluid retention and impending decompensation, per NICE guidelines. It is often the earliest sign and warrants prompt diuretic adjustment to prevent acute heart failure. The other findings are also concerning but weight gain is the specific threshold marker.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 35%) and moderate chronic kidney disease (eGFR 45 ml/min) is on ramipril 5 mg daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, and furosemide 40 mg daily. He attends a routine check-up. He feels well, but his blood test results show: sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 5.9 mmol/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 150 µmol/L (baseline 130). Which single finding is most concerning and requires urgent action?
questions.explanation: A potassium of 5.9 mmol/L (hyperkalaemia) is a life-threatening complication, especially in patients on renin-angiotensin system inhibitors (ramipril). It can cause cardiac arrhythmias and requires immediate interruption of the offending drug and emergency management. The other values are mildly abnormal but not immediately dangerous.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known HFrEF (LVEF 25%) and atrial fibrillation (controlled on bisoprolol and warfarin) is brought to the emergency department after collapsing at home. He is now alert but feels dizzy and nauseated. On examination, pulse is rapid and irregular, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg. ECG shows a broad-complex tachycardia (QRS duration 160 ms) at a rate of 180 bpm, irregularly irregular. Which single finding most strongly suggests a life-threatening arrhythmia requiring immediate treatment?
questions.explanation: A broad QRS complex (160 ms) suggests ventricular tachycardia (VT), a life-threatening arrhythmia that requires immediate cardioversion. The irregular rhythm could be atrial fibrillation with aberrancy or pre-excited AF, but VT is more likely in this setting and is a 'can't-miss' diagnosis. The other findings are supportive but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with HFrEF (LVEF 30%) secondary to ischaemic heart disease presents with sudden-onset, severe breathlessness and pink frothy sputum. On examination, she is distressed, unable to speak in full sentences, pulse 120 bpm, blood pressure 160/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 36 breaths/min, oxygen saturations 85% on room air, and she has widespread crackles and wheeze. Which single finding most clearly marks this as a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate non-invasive ventilation?
options: Inability to speak in full sentences
questions.explanation: Pink frothy sputum is a classic sign of acute pulmonary oedema with proteinaceous fluid in the alveoli, indicating severe cardiogenic pulmonary oedema requiring immediate positive pressure ventilation. While all options indicate severity, frothy sputum is pathognomonic for this condition and a clear red flag.
